title: The Fortune Teller of Fuxing Road
content:
The bell above the door chimed at exactly 11:47 PM. Chen Wei didn't need to look up from her tea to know who it was — the bell only rang three times, and always in the same rhythm. A heartbeat in brass.

Her shop, Red Lotus Antiques, occupied the ground floor of a crumbling French Concession building on Fuxing Road. The walls were lined with shelves that sagged under the weight of jade figurines, bronze mirrors, and scrolls so old that touching them with bare hands was technically illegal.

The man who entered wore a bespoke charcoal suit and shoes that cost more than Chen Wei's monthly rent. Silver hair swept back. Eyes that had already decided everything.

"Mr. Wang," Chen Wei said. "You're early."

Li Wei Wang — CEO of Wang Group Holdings, thirty-seventh richest person in China — stopped mid-stride. He had not given his name. He had not made an appointment.

"How?" he asked.

Chen Wei set down her teacup. "Please. Sit."

He sat. Later, he would wonder why he had obeyed.

"You know why you're here," Chen Wei said. It wasn't a question.

"I want to know when I will die."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Any other fortune teller would have recoiled. Chen Wei simply nodded.

"That's why you came. But it's not why you're here. Your question is wrong. Would you like to ask the right one?"

Li Wei Wang's jaw tightened. "What's the right question?"

"Who wants you dead."

The silence that followed was deafening. Outside, Shanghai blazed with neon and noise. Inside, time had frozen.

"Someone wants me dead?"

"Everyone wants you dead, Mr. Wang. You've made enough enemies. But only one of them will succeed. And that particular enemy has been waiting for longer than you have been alive."

"How long do I have?"

"That's a different question." Chen Wei smiled — not a comforting smile. "There is something I need to know. About a research project that was shut down in 1997. About a laboratory built over a temple, and a thing that was uncovered in the foundations."

Li Wei Wang went pale. The year 1997. The year Wang Group had almost collapsed. The year he had made a deal with something he still didn't understand.

"How do you—"

"Because we're connected, Mr. Wang. Your past and my future. Your father's sins and my mother's warnings. This isn't a fortune. This is a debt. And tonight, you begin to pay it back."

The bell chimed, unbidden, three times.

"Come back tomorrow at midnight. I'll have the first answer for you. The when, not the why. The why will take longer."

title: Three Generations of Secrets
content:
Chen Wei locked the shop door at 1 AM and climbed the narrow stairs to her apartment. The walls were covered in maps — not tourist maps, but genealogical maps tracing bloodlines across centuries.

The largest of these maps belonged to the Wang family. Li Wei Wang's family.

She sat at her desk and pulled out the leather journal that had been her mother's, and her grandmother's before that. The first entry was dated 1945. The last was dated June 2, 1997.

October 19, 1945: "The excavation on Wulumuqi Road has stopped. The workers refuse to go any deeper. They say the temple predates anything they have ever seen — Tang Dynasty at least, maybe older."

March 14, 1997: "The laboratory has been built directly over the site. Wang Group funded the entire project. They say they're researching geothermal energy, but I have been seeing things in the basement."

June 2, 1997: "I have made a terrible mistake. The thing we uncovered is not a temple. It was never a temple. The carvings on the foundation stones are not prayers — they are warnings. And the date they warn about is not in the past. It is in the future."

The journal entries stopped after June 2nd. On June 3rd, the laboratory burned. Thirty-seven people died. Chen Wei's mother survived because she'd been sent home early — pregnancy complications, the official report said.

But Chen Wei's grandmother had told a different story: "Your mother was never supposed to be in that laboratory. Wang needed someone who could read the old script. And when she read it — when she understood what they had found — she set the fire herself."

Chen Wei closed the journal. Li Wei Wang's death — she had seen it the moment he walked through the door — was July 15th. Three weeks from now.

The thing in the foundations. It was still there. They never destroyed it. They just built over it.

And now, somehow, it was waking up.

title: The First Client Returns
content:
Li Wei Wang returned the next night at exactly midnight. He looked like a man who hadn't slept — his tie was crooked, his silver hair disheveled.

"The woman in the lab. Your mother. She was pregnant. Did the child survive?"

Chen Wei studied him for a long moment. "Yes. She survived."

"Where is she? If she knows anything about what happened—"

"She knows everything." Chen Wei's voice went cold. "She knows who built the lab. She knows who authorized the dig. She knows who ordered her mother to translate the carvings, even after she warned them the script was dangerous."

Li Wei Wang's face crumpled. "I didn't mean for anyone to die."

"The fire was your salvation." Chen Wei opened the leather journal. "My mother wrote: 'The temple was built to seal something. Not a tomb. Not a treasure vault. Something alive. Something that the Tang Dynasty cultivators could not destroy but could only contain. The seal is broken now. I broke it. Wang made me break it.'"

"Cultivators? That's mythology."

"Your company has spent thirty years studying mythology, Mr. Wang. You have a division devoted to nothing else." Chen Wei rolled up her left sleeve. A mark covered her forearm — a spiral of black lines that seemed to move when you weren't looking directly at them. "This appeared the night I turned eighteen. The same night my grandmother died. The same night the lights flickered in every building within a kilometer of the old laboratory site."

She pulled her sleeve back down. "July 15th. Three weeks from now. That's when the seal breaks. That's when you die. Unless we reseal it. Together."

"Together?"

"You have resources I don't. I have knowledge you don't. If we succeed, you live. If we fail — you were going to die anyway."

Li Wei Wang stared at her hand, extended toward him. Then, slowly, he shook it.

"Tell me what you need," he said.

title: The Laboratory Beneath Shanghai
content:
They drove to the old laboratory site at 3 AM. Zhang, Li Wei Wang's driver and bodyguard, took them through the industrial outskirts of Shanghai, past warehouses and streets with no names.

The laboratory had been built directly over what the original surveyors called a "geological anomaly." The official records said the site was abandoned.

"You kept security," Chen Wei observed as a uniformed guard checked their credentials.

"The research never stopped. After the fire, I had the building sealed. But I couldn't just walk away."

"Your father started this in 1945."

"He was obsessed. He never told me what he was looking for. After he died in 1968, I inherited the project. I thought it was archaeology. Cultural preservation. I didn't know the truth until 1997."

"And in 1997?"

"The carvings your mother translated. They described a cultivator from the Tang Dynasty — a rogue who discovered immortality by consuming the life force of others. They called him the 'Devourer of Souls.' The records say he was executed. The carvings said something else entirely."

"They trapped him," Chen Wei said. "They couldn't kill him, so they built a prison."

"And we opened it."

They walked through the fire-damaged corridors to an office marked "Dr. Liu Xia — Senior Archaeologist." Chen Wei's mother's office.

On the desk, untouched by fire or decay, lay a leather portfolio. Inside were her mother's final translations. And tucked between the pages, a single sheet covered in handwritten notes.

At the bottom, in shaking characters: "For my daughter. When the time comes, you will know what to do. — Mom"

Chen Wei read the instructions twice. Then she looked up at Li Wei Wang, her eyes blazing.

"We need five things," she said. "And we have three weeks to find them."

title: The Five Seals
content:
Back in the cramped apartment above Red Lotus Antiques, Chen Wei spread her mother's instructions across every available surface.

"Five seals," she said. "Each created by a different Tang Dynasty cultivator. Together they form a spiritual lock that can contain any entity — even an immortal. But the lock requires maintenance. Every five hundred years, all five seals must be recharged, or the prison weakens."

"And they haven't been touched since the Tang Dynasty."

"Exactly. The cultivators scattered them across China to prevent anyone from finding them all. My mother spent ten years tracking them down." Chen Wei tapped the map. "She found four: Mount Emei in Sichuan, Longmen Grottoes in Henan, a village in the Wuyi Mountains of Fujian, and Dunhuang in Gansu."

"That leaves one missing."

"The fifth seal moves. It was bound to a bloodline, passed from mother to daughter for fifty-three generations." Chen Wei touched her left forearm. "I carry it. This mark isn't a curse. It's a responsibility."

Li Wei Wang stared at her. "You're the fifth seal."

"The fifth seal's keeper. And it's been weakening — which means the other four must be in worse shape."

"That's a lot of ground to cover in three weeks."

"Then you'd better make some phone calls. I assume the thirty-seventh richest man in China has a private jet?"

Li Wei Wang actually smiled. "I have three. We leave tomorrow."

After he left, Chen Wei sat alone in her apartment, her mother's final letter clutched in her hands. The mark on her arm burned steadily.

Somewhere beneath Shanghai, something ancient stirred in its prison.

She picked up her phone and dialed a number she had never called before. A voice answered on the second ring.

"Cousin. It's time. Meet me at Hongqiao Airport tomorrow morning. Bring the family sword."

The voice was silent for a moment. Then, quietly: "I've been waiting thirty years for this call. I'll be there."

Chen Wei hung up. The bell above the shop door chimed, though the shop was locked and empty.

"I'm coming, Mother," she thought. "I'm finally coming."
